Related Sources [[S018-the-heroic-struggle]] — Appendix 7 contains his full letters Di Yisurim fun Lubavitcher Rebben in Soviet Russland (Riga 1930) Related Entities [[rayatz]] — The Rebbe he served [[mmm]] — Future Rebbe who also accompanied to Kostroma [[E020-michoel-dworkin]] — Another chassid who demonstrated mesirus nefesh Last Updated: 2026-04-13

title: Reb Dovid (the Butcher)
content: Reb Dovid (the Butcher) Lubavitch merchant known for his piety and optimism. A key figure in young Yosef Yitzchak's childhood, Reb Dovid was one of the first beneficiaries of the future Rebbe's chesed (humanitarian assistance) loan fund. Background Location: Lubavitch Occupation: Butcher and livestock merchant Character: Known for his beaming face and faith in G-d The Loan Fund In the summer of 1891, young Yosef Yitzchak (age 11) had saved over 30 rubles from studying Mishnah. Following his father's suggestion to use the money for chesed, he began making microloans to fellow Jews. Reb Dovid was among the first recipients. Yosef Yitzchak would later recount: "He was carrying a calf on his shoulders, a lamb in his arms, and a basket of chickens hanging in front of him. His face beamed as he exclaimed, 'I hope G-d will give me a decent profit.'" Relationship with Young Yosef Yitzchak Reb Dovid was well acquainted with Yosef Yitzchak, who had been staking him with microloans from his fund so that Dovid could purchase livestock from nearby farms and resell it in town. Sources [[S018-the-heroic-struggle]] [[S002-undaunted-chapter1]] Last Updated: 2026-04-13

title: Rabbi Elchanan Dovber Morosow
content: Rabbi Elchanan Dovber Morosow Secretary and close chassid of the Frierdiker Rebbe, Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn. Role Served as the Rebbe's personal secretary Responsible for managing the Rebbe's correspondence and schedule Present during key events including the 1927 crisis The Warning (1924) After consultations in Moscow and the Rebbe's election as leader of the organization, Reb Elchanan Dovber met the Rebbe on the journey back to Rostov with an urgent warning: "agents of the local G.P.U. were waiting to imprison him and send him into exile for his work to strengthen religion." This warning prompted the Rebbe to: Turn around on the road Stay in a small village near Moscow until the fury subsided Return to Rostov on the 11th of Nissan Eventually agree to leave Rostov voluntarily for Leningrad Sources [[S018-the-heroic-struggle]] Last Updated: 2026-04-13

title: Rabbi Dovid Tevel Katzenelenbogen
content: Rabbi Dovid Tevel Katzenelenbogen Chief Rabbi of Leningrad during the 1920s. Position Chief Rabbi of Leningrad Led the Jewish community during the period of intense Soviet persecution Relationship with the Rebbe The Rebbe was in communication with Rabbi Katzenelenbogen and found himself in "deep distress" because of their discussions. The "General Assembly" Dispute Anti-religious Jews had scheduled a "General Assembly" of Leningrad's communal leaders. They had: Deceived the Chief Rabbi into siding with them Created false issues of personal conflict to undermine traditional Judaism The Rebbe opposed this assembly, recognizing it as a scheme to undermine traditional Jewish leadership. This opposition came on the very night of his arrest (14 Sivan 5687 / June 14, 1927). Historical Context During this period: The Yevsektzia was actively working to destroy Jewish religious life Traditional rabbis were under constant threat of arrest Any Jewish leader who cooperated with anti-religious forces was seen as betraying the community Sources [[S018-the-heroic-struggle]] Also referenced as [[E014-katznelenbogen]] in earlier sources Last Updated: 2026-04-13

title: Spalerno Prison
content: Spalerno Prison The prison in Leningrad where the Frierdiker Rebbe was incarcerated during his 1927 imprisonment. 1927 Imprisonment The Rebbe Rayatz wrote a maamar of Chassidus on Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, 5687 (1927) during his incarceration at Spalerno Prison. This maamar was published as a separate booklet. Conditions at Spalerno From the Rebbe's account: Midnight arrest: 2:15 AM on 15 Sivan 5687 Duration: 18 days, 11 hours, 15 minutes of imprisonment Interrogation and intimidation by GPU officers Humiliation of prison routine The Rebbe described these as days of "affliction of the body and suffering of the spirit" Spiritual Activity During Imprisonment Despite the conditions, the Rebbe engaged in spiritual work: Wrote the maamar on Rosh Chodesh Tammuz Maintained his composure and faith ("I have not feared them, I do not fear them, and I shall not fear them") Viewed the suffering as "luminous days" that elevated his spiritual level Related [[E011-rayatz-arrest-1927]] — The arrest and imprisonment [[E001-rayatz]] — The prisoner [[gpu]] — The secret police operating the prison [[C001-yud-beis-tammuz]] — Liberation day

title: Kostroma (Exile)
content: Kostroma The city to which the Frierdiker Rebbe was sentenced to three years of exile in 1927, after the capital charge was quashed. The Exile Sentence On 3 Tammuz 5687 (1927), after 18 days of imprisonment, the capital charge was quashed and commuted to: Three years of exile in Kostroma Located on the Volga River A remote city, far from Jewish communities Timeline to Kostroma Date Event 3 Tammuz, 1:30 PM Capital charge quashed; exile sentence 3 Tammuz, 7:30 PM Departure by train from Leningrad 4 Tammuz Arrival in Kostroma 4-12 Tammuz 9 days and 17 hours in exile 12 Tammuz (Yud-Beis) Notification of impending release 13 Tammuz (Yud-Gimmel) Actual liberation (12:30 PM) The Farewell Message Before boarding the train to exile on 3 Tammuz, the Rebbe stood on the train step and delivered his famous farewell message to the chassidim who had accompanied him to the station: "We must always bear in mind that prisons and hard-labor camps are transient, whereas the Torah, its mitzvos, and the Jewish people, are eternal." Liberation The exile lasted less than two weeks. On Yud-Beis Tammuz (12 Tammuz), the Rebbe was informed of his impending release. On Yud-Gimmel Tammuz (13 Tammuz) at 12:30 PM, he was liberated — a day celebrated as Yud-Beis Tammuz in Chabad. Related [[E011-rayatz-arrest-1927]] — Full timeline of arrest, imprisonment, exile, and liberation [[C001-yud-beis-tammuz]] — The holiday celebrating this liberation [[E001-rayatz]] — The Rebbe Rayatz

title: Undaunted - Preface
content: Undaunted - Preface Overview The preface introduces Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn, the sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe (the Rayatz), as a pivotal figure who stood at the apex of Jewish history during tumultuous times. It sets the framework for understanding his life as a journey through four major historical challenges. Key Themes Four Historical Eras Czarist Russia — Early years facing oppression Communist Soviet Union — Rise of communism seeking to eradicate Jewish traditions Westernized Europe — Adapting Chabad Chasidism outside Eastern Europe America — Reimagining Jewish life amid assimilation, laying foundation for renaissance Core Character Traits Undaunted courage — Bucked conventional wisdom repeatedly Adaptability — Successfully started anew in different countries Spiritual rootedness — First and foremost rooted in deep spiritual values Torua scholar — Left 200+ volumes of maamarim, talks, and writings Activist — Organizer and leader despite health issues, limited finances Compassion — Boundless compassion for Jews everywhere (even Morocco) Sense of mission — Instilled by his father, the Rashab Key Quote "Without a yeshivah there is no life." — The Rebbe Historical Significance The preframe frames the Rayatz as: One of the most consequential figures of the modern Jewish era A godly man who created a path for a bright Jewish future A leader who remained "undaunted" through unparalleled disruption and transformation Naming Conventions Explained The preface clarifies how the Rebbe is referred to across different life stages: Period Name Years Childhood Yosef Yitzchak 1880-1893 Before Rebbehood The Rayatz 1893-1920 As Rebbe The Rebbe 1920-1950 Also noted: Chasidim commonly refer to the Rayatz as the Friedeke Rebbe (Yiddish for "Previous Rebbe"). Key Family Members Mentioned [[The Rashab]] — Rabbi Shalom Dovber Schneersohn, fifth Rebbe, father of the Rayatz [[The Raza]] — Rabbi Zalman Aharon, brother of the Rashab [[The Rashag]] — Rabbi Shmaryahu Gurary, eldest son-in-law [[The Ramash]] — Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, seventh Rebbe (son-in-law and successor) Source Context This summary is based on the preface of "Undaunted," which serves as the introduction to a biography that draws on thousands of letters, public addresses, and first-person accounts to let the Rebbe's own voice resonate. Continue to: [[S002-undaunted-chapter1]]

title: Undaunted - Chapter 1: Lubavitch
content: Undaunted - Chapter 1: Lubavitch Overview Chapter 1 covers the early life of young Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn in Lubavitch, culminating in his first arrest at age 11 for defending a Jew from police brutality. This incident established the pattern of fearless defense that would define his life. Key Events 1891: First Arrest (Age 11) Date: June 29, 1891 (Yarmarka market day) Location: Marketplace, Lubavitch Incident: Young Yosef Yitzchak defends Reb Dovid the butcher from police sergeant brutality Outcome: Arrested, imprisoned in volost station Significance: First of seven arrests; demonstrated fearless defense of Jews Context: Lubavitch as Chabad Capital 1813: Rabbi Dovber (Mitteler Rebbe) settles in Lubavitch 102 years: Lubavitch serves as Chabad capital (1813-1915) Population: ~2,000 residents, doubled during market fairs Birth and Early Life Birth (1880) Date: June 30, 1880 (12 Tamuz 5640) Location: Lubavitch, White Russia Parents: Rabbi Shalom Dovber (Rashab) and Rebbetzin Shterna Sara The Prophetic Dream (1879) Rebbetzin Shterna Sara's dream on Simchat Torah 1879: Overlooked for blessing by Rabbi Shmuel (Maharash) Dreams of three Jews who promise her a son Identified as first three Chabad Rebbes (Alter Rebbe, Mitteler Rebbe, Tzemach Tzedek) Prophetic fulfillment: Yosef Yitzchak born the following year Bar Mitzvah (1893) Date: Monday, June 26, 1893 (Tamuz 26) Location: Lubavitch Activities: 6:00 AM: Mikveh immersion Prayed at gravesites of Rabbi Menachem Mendel and Rabbi Shmuel Weeklong festivities, maamarim, blessings Leadership Transitions Covered Maharash's Passing (1882) Date: Shortly after Yom Kippur 1882 Age: 48 Last words: "You will rise up thirty-two steps in a physical dimension and I will rise up thirty-two steps in a spiritual way" Leadership crisis: Two sons (Raza and Rashab) initially refuse leadership Rashab Accepts Leadership (1893) Date: Rosh HaShanah 1893 Age: 32 Significance: After 11 years of hesitation, fully assumes role Era: "Era of rebirth of Lubavitch" Rashab Becomes Rebbe (1920) Yosef Yitzchak succeeds his father at age 40 Becomes sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe Lubavitch: Historical Significance Timeline 1813: Rabbi Dovber settles in Lubavitch after Napoleon's invasion 1915, October 24 (17 Cheshvan): Rashab and Rayatz flee German invasion 1936: Last synagogue closed by communist authorities 1941, July 21: Nazis seize Lubavitch 1941, November 4: 483 Jews murdered; ghetto destroyed Key Themes Fearless Defense — Standing up against oppression begins at age 11 Lubavitch as Home — Spiritual and physical center of Chabad Leadership Crisis — Pattern of reluctant leadership acceptance Divine Providence — Prophetic dreams and fulfilled blessings People Introduced Person Role Significance [[Reb Dovid the Butcher]] Local merchant Victim of police brutality; defended by young Yosef Yitzchak [[Shimon Rabinowitz]] Childhood friend With Yosef Yitzchak during arrest incident [[Rebbetzin Shterna Sara]] Mother of Rayatz Had prophetic dream of son's birth [[Rabbi Shmuel (Maharash)]] Fourth Rebbe Died 1882; created leadership crisis Sources Chapter 1 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S001-undaunted-preface]] — Preface Next: [[S003-undaunted-chapter2]] — Chapter 2

title: Undaunted - Chapter 2: Tomchei Temimim
content: Undaunted - Chapter 2: Tomchei Temimim Overview Chapter 2 covers the founding and early development of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim, the revolutionary educational institution that integrated Talmud and Chasidic philosophy. The yeshiva was founded as the Rayatz's wedding gift from his father, marking the beginning of his leadership in Jewish education. Key Events Engagement (1896) Date: June 9, 1896 (28 Sivan, 5646) Location: Bolivke (summer resort) Event: Rayatz engaged to second cousin Nechama Dina Schneersohn Celebration: Lasted a week Significance: Marriage connection chosen over wealthier prospects Wedding (1897) Date: September 10, 1897 (13 Elul, 5657) Location: Lubavitch Ceremony: Friday afternoon in courtyard of main synagogue Details: Rayatz donned shtreimel for first time Celebration: Continued Sunday evening in the zal Significance: Led directly to founding of Tomchei Temilim Founding of Tomchei Temimim (1897) Date: September 12, 1897 (15 Elul, 5657) Occasion: Wedding reception meeting Participants: 50 distinguished wedding guests Catalyst: Rayatz's wedding gift request to his father Revolution: Integration of Talmud and chasidic philosophy Significance: First systematic chasidic yeshivah curriculum First Day of Studies (1897) Date: September 15, 1897 (18 Elul) Significance: Birthday of Baal Shem Tov and Alter Rebbe First mashpia: Rabbi Shmuel Gronem Students: Original group of 18 Rayatz Appointed Director (1898) Date: September 11, 1898 (24 Elul, 5659) Age: 18 years old Initiative: Group of Chasidim led by Rabbi Asher Grossman Process: appealed to Rashab when Rayatz demurred Significance: Beginning of Rayatz's leadership in education Mission Statement (1900) Date: Simchat Torah 5661 Metaphor: Students as soldiers of King David Mission: Disseminate chasidic teachings with self-sacrifice Requirement: Divorce from worldly concerns Hebron Branch (1911) Decision: 1910 to open branch in Eretz Yisrael Property: Beit Romano purchased (1908) Team: Led by Rabbi Zalman Havlin Significance: First international expansion; first chasidic yeshivah in Holy Land Key People Person Role Significance [[Nechama Dina Schneersohn]] Wife of Rayatz Second cousin; marriage led to yeshiva founding Rabbi Zalman Havlin Scholar Led Hebron branch team Rabbi Shmuel Gronem Mashpia First teacher at Tomchei Temilim Rabbi Asher Grossman Chasid Led initiative to appoint Rayatz as director Moshe Rosenblum Participant At founding meeting Revolutionary Concept Educational Integration Tomchei Temilim was revolutionary because it: Integrated Talmud study with chasidic philosophy Systematized chasidic teachings in a curriculum Created a new model for Jewish education Produced leaders who could disseminate chasidic teachings Student Mission The Rashab defined students as: Soldiers of King David Self-sacrificing for the mission Divorced from worldly concerns Dedicated to disseminating chasidic philosophy Timeline Date Event June 9, 1896 Engagement to Nechama Dina Sept 10, 1897 Wedding Sept 12, 1897 Founding of Tomchei Temilim Sept 15, 1897 First day of studies Sept 11, 1898 Rayatz appointed director (age 18) 1900 Mission statement on Simchat Torah 1908 Beit Romano purchased in Hebron 1910 Decision to open Hebron branch 1911 Hebron branch established Sources Chapter 2 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S002-undaunted-chapter1]] — Chapter 1: Lubavitch Next: [[S004-undaunted-chapter3]] — Chapter 3

title: Undaunted - Chapter 3: The Secret Covenant
content: Undaunted - Chapter 3: The Secret Covenant Overview Chapter 3 covers the transition of leadership from the Rashab to the Rayatz in 1920, including the private "secret covenant" between father and son that would define the Rayatz's approach to leadership. Key Events Rashab's Passing and Burial (1920) Date: Late Saturday night (passing), Sunday afternoon (funeral/burial) Location: Rostov Attendance: All local Jews, yeshivah students, elders Rayatz's response: Led evening prayers in tears Significance: End of an era; leadership transition begins The Secret Covenant After the burial, the Rayatz confided in Berel Rivkin about his father's warnings he hadn't comprehended. The Rashab had prepared his son for leadership through private instructions, creating a "secret covenant" between them. First Maamar (End of Shivah) Timing: Shabbat afternoon, end of week of mourning Location: Rashab's private study in Rostov Participant: Berel Rivkin (private delivery) Significance: First step toward accepting leadership Passover Maamarim (1920) First day of Passover: Private maamar to Rivkin, others listened from adjoining room End of Passover: Maamar with Chasidim present Description: "Exactly like when the Rebbe Rashab said a maamar" Significance: Public acceptance of leadership role Yeshivah Relocation (Winter 1920) From: Kremenchuk To: Rostov Reason: Fear of starvation Significance: Yeshivah under Rayatz's direct supervision Typhus Illness (November 1920) Date: November 8, 1920 (28 Marcheshvan 5681) Context: Typhus epidemic in Rostov Severity: Near-fatal; unable to lead services Recovery: Had strength to say maamar on following Shabbat Significance: Health challenge during early leadership Key People Person Role Significance [[Berel Rivkin]] Confidant Recipient of first maamarim; witness to transition The Secret Covenant The Rashab had prepared his son for leadership through: Private warnings about passing that son didn't initially comprehend Private instructions to deliver maamarim Father-son bond that defined the transition The Rayatz fulfilled these instructions by: Delivering maamarim privately first Gradually expanding to public delivery Maintaining the covenant's privacy Timeline Date Event Late Sat 1920 Rashab's passing Sun 1920 Funeral and burial Shabbat (end of shivah) First private maamar to Rivkin Passover 1920 Maamarim with expanding audience Winter 1920 Yeshivah moves to Rostov Nov 8, 1920 Rayatz contracts typhus Sources Chapter 3 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S003-undaunted-chapter2]] — Chapter 2: Tomchei Temilim Next: [[S005-undaunted-chapter4]] — Chapter 4

title: Undaunted - Chapter 4: Armed Men at Midnight
content: Undaunted - Chapter 4: Armed Men at Midnight Overview Chapter 4 covers the infamous 1927 arrest of the Rebbe Rayatz by the GPU (Soviet secret police) — the midnight arrest that began a 19-day ordeal of imprisonment, interrogation, and ultimate liberation. This event became a defining moment in Chabad history. The Arrest Date and Time Date: June 15, 1927 (15 Sivan 5687) Time: Between midnight and 1:00 AM Location: 22 Mochovaya Street, Leningrad Perpetrators: Armed GPU agents (Soviet secret police) Family Present Rebbetzin Nechama Dina Daughters: Chaya Mushka, Sheina, Chana, Mushka (Moussia) Son-in-law: Rashag (Rabbi Shmaryahu Gourary) The 19-Day Ordeal Initial Detention Transported to Shpalerka prison Interrogations with death threats Tefillin confiscated (later returned after hunger strike) International Pressure World Jewish community mobilized International protests and diplomatic pressure Appeals to Soviet authorities Sentence and Commutation Initial sentence: Death penalty Commuted to: Exile in Solovki Final sentence: 3 years exile in Kostroma Liberation Date: July 3, 1927 (3 Tamuz) Duration: 19 days in captivity Result: Complete liberation on 12 Tamuz Key Dates Date Event June 14, 1927 Midnight arrest at 22 Mochovaya Street June 15, 1927 Transported to Shpalerka prison June 16, 1927 First interrogation with death threat June 17, 1927 Tefillin returned after hunger strike June 21, 1927 Second interrogation June 25, 1927 Postcard reaches family June 29, 1927 Death sentence commuted to Solovki exile June 30, 1927 Sentence changed to 3-year Kostroma exile July 3, 1927 (3 Tamuz) Release after 19 days July 3, 1927 Departure for Kostroma exile July 12, 1927 (12 Tamuz) Full liberation from exile Historical Significance This arrest was: The most severe of seven arrests A defining moment in Soviet-Jewish relations Demonstration of fearless resistance to communism Catalyst for international Jewish solidarity Foundation for annual 12-13 Tamuz commemorations Key People Person Role Significance [[Nechama Dina Schneersohn]] Rebbetzin Present during arrest; supported throughout ordeal [[Rashag]] Son-in-law Helped during arrest period Chaya Mushka Daughter Witnessed arrest; later married seventh Rebbe Rabbi Eliyahu Chaim Althaus Documentarian Letters provide primary source Sources Chapter 4 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S004-undaunted-chapter3]] — Chapter 3: The Secret Covenant Next: [[S006-undaunted-chapter5]] — Chapter 5

title: Undaunted - Chapter 5: Exile
content: Undaunted - Chapter 5: Exile Overview Chapter 5 covers the period of exile in Kostroma following the 1927 arrest — the time between liberation from prison and the final release from exile sentence. Key Events Release from Prison Date: July 3, 1927 (3 Tamuz) Context: After 19 days in captivity Status: Released but still under exile sentence Departure for Kostroma Timing: July 3, 1927 (same day as release) Mode: Train journey Sentencing: 3 years exile in Kostroma Conditions: Remote location, distance from Jewish community Final Liberation Date: July 12, 1927 (12 Tamuz) Event: Complete release from exile sentence Significance: End of 1927 ordeal; return to leadership Historical Context GPU tactics: Release followed by exile was common Soviet technique International pressure: Continued advocacy contributed to release Health impact: 19-day imprisonment + travel took physical toll Sources Chapter 5 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S005-undaunted-chapter4]] — Chapter 4: Armed Men at Midnight Next: [[S007-undaunted-chapter6]] — Chapter 6

title: Undaunted - Chapter 6: The Underground Years
content: Undaunted - Chapter 6: The Underground Years Overview Chapter 6 covers the period of underground Jewish educational activities under Soviet persecution — the secret network of schools, teachers, and students who maintained Torah learning despite government prohibition. Key Themes Underground Education Network Secret schools across Soviet Union Hidden curriculum of Torah and Chasidic teachings Teachers and students risked arrest Thousands of children participated Soviet Persecution Government ban on religious education GPU surveillance of Jewish communities Arrests of educators Closing of synagogues and religious institutions Tomchei Temilim Underground Yeshiva continued operating in secret Students disguised as secular students Maamarim taught in private homes Network extended across multiple cities Key Activities Secret teaching: Torah study hidden from authorities Mock summer camps: Disguised religious education Correspondence courses: Remote learning system Teacher training: Preparing new generation of educators Historical Significance This period demonstrated: Resilience of Jewish education under persecution Creative adaptation to Soviet constraints Commitment to Torah transmission Foundation for post-war Chabad revival Sources Chapter 6 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S006-undaunted-chapter5]] — Chapter 5: Exile Next: [[S008-undaunted-chapter7]] — Chapter 7

title: Undaunted - Chapter 7: The Voyage
content: Undaunted - Chapter 7: The Voyage Overview Chapter 7 covers the Rebbe's yearlong journey from 1929-1930 — travels to America and Eretz Yisrael to secure funding for Russian Jews and visit the graves of righteous predecessors. Key Events Decision to Travel (1929) Crisis: JDC suspended funding for Rebbe's work in Russia JDC shift: Partnered with Communist regime instead Rebbe's response: "The disheartening news regarding the JDC budget compels me to travel successfully to the US" Consultation: Sought guidance from Rabbi Chaim Ozer and the Chafetz Chaim Journey to America Route: Riga → Eretz Yisrael → America Purpose: Two dilemmas - help Russian Jews, visit graves of tzadikim Arrival: March 1929 in New York Harbor Eretz Yisrael Visit Visited graves of righteous ancestors Spiritual rejuvenation Connection to holy sites American Visit (1929-1930) Met with JDC leadership in New York Fundraising for Russian Jewish communities Established American support network Strengthened traditional Judaism in US Key People Person Role Significance Yehuda Lokshin Chabad director in NY Recipient of first indication of travel plans Rabbi Chaim Ozer Torah leader Encouraged the trip Chafetz Chaim Torah sage Encouraged the trip Historical Context 1927: Rebbe's arrest and exile created crisis for Russian Jews 1928: JDC shifted support to Communist regime 1929: Lunacharsky declared "We shall combat the Schneersohnovschina" Great Depression: Emerging economic crisis limited fundraising potential Outcomes The journey established: American Chabad support network Direct funding channels for Russian Jews Foundation for future American relationship Preparation for eventual 1940 immigration Sources Chapter 7 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S007-undaunted-chapter6]] — Chapter 6 Next: [[S009-undaunted-chapter8]] — Chapter 8

title: Undaunted - Chapter 8: Poland - Starting Anew
content: Undaunted - Chapter 8: Poland - Starting Anew Overview Chapter 8 covers the period from 1930 onward when the Rebbe established his base in Poland and focused on rebuilding Chabad's yeshivah system while facing rising challenges — health issues, economic depression, and the ominous rise of Nazi Germany. Key Events Return from America (1930) Timing: Just before High Holidays Location: Riga as starting point Crossroads decision: Where to focus efforts next Strategic Situation (1930) East: Millions of Jews behind Iron Curtain under Stalin West: Poland — center of European religious life Challenge: Reinvigorate Chabad yeshivah system Global: Scattered emerging communities needing nurturing Poland as Base Location: Otwock, near Warsaw Focus: Rebuilding Tomchei Temimim yeshivah network Goal: Birth new generations of Chasidim Challenges Faced Health: Serious health challenges for Rebbe Economy: Great Depression limited resources Politics: Rising militarism in Germany Resources: Major financial needs across all fronts Historical Context Poland in 1930s Center of European religious Jewish life Home to largest yeshivah network Relatively safe compared to Soviet Union Growing but ultimately temporary haven American Support 1929 visit broadened support base Depression limited fundraising potential Foundation established for future relationship Key Themes Rebuilding — Creating new center after Russian exile Education — Focus on yeshivah as foundation Health challenges — Physical struggles during leadership Gathering storm — Nazi rise threatening European Jewry Outcomes This period established: Strong Polish Chabad community Revitalized yeshivah system Network of students who would later spread globally Foundation for eventual wartime rescue efforts Sources Chapter 8 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S008-undaunted-chapter7]] — Chapter 7 Next: [[S010-undaunted-chapter9]] — Chapter 9

title: Undaunted - Chapter 9: The World is Shattered
content: Undaunted - Chapter 9: The World is Shattered Overview Chapter 9 covers the outbreak of World War II in September 1939 and the miraculous rescue operation that saved the Rebbe from Nazi-occupied Poland — a dramatic story involving diplomats, intelligence agents, and Chabad activists across multiple countries. Key Events Final Meeting Before War (August 26, 1939) Location: Otwock, Poland Participants: Rebbe and Rabbi Yisrael Jacobson Topic: Rebbe's planned relocation to US Rebbe's words: "In about half a year, with G-d's assistance, we will come to America" Five days later: Germany invaded Poland (September 1, 1939) War Breaks Out (September 1, 1939) 4:45 AM: German battleship shelled Gdańsk Morning: Panzer tanks invaded Poland 8:00 AM: German planes bombed Otwock Context: Yeshivah students celebrating founding farbrengen at dawn Twelve-Week Ordeal The Rebbe endured twelve weeks under Nazi rule in Warsaw — suffering he later said exceeded even his Soviet imprisonment. The Rescue Operation A multinational effort involving: Brooklyn: Chabad activists launching campaign Washington: Government officials pressured Riga: Chasidim coordinating escape Berlin: Diplomats negotiating Abwehr: German intelligence officers helping Wehrmacht: Soldiers of Jewish background assisting Escape from Warsaw Rosh Hashanah: Bombing forced evacuation to medical clinic Danger: Nazi regime actively hunting Rebbe American intervention: US officials pressured for release German helpers: Abwehr agents including Bloch escorted Rebbe Journey to Freedom Escape route: Warsaw → Nazi Germany → Latvian border → Riga Arrival in Riga: December 17, 1939 (5 Tevet 5700) Mood: "We were very, very happy" to escape Nazi regime Key People Person Role Significance Rabbi Yisrael Jacobson US Chabad leader Last meeting before war; launched rescue campaign Rabbi Yechezkel Feigin Secretary Documented the ordeal; managed precious manuscripts Colonel Bloch Abwehr officer Escorted Rebbe across Nazi Germany; died 1945 Wilhelm Canaris Abwehr chief Approved rescue; hanged 1945 for anti-Hitler plot Rabbi Shmuel Levitin US leader Coordinated rescue efforts Historical Significance This rescue demonstrated: International Chabad network effectiveness Divine providence in survival Complexity of wartime escape Foundation for future American leadership Sources Chapter 9 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S009-undaunted-chapter8]] — Chapter 8 Next: [[S011-undaunted-chapter10]] — Chapter 10

title: Undaunted - Chapter 10: America Iz Nisht Aderesh
content: Undaunted - Chapter 10: America Iz Nisht Aderesh Overview Chapter 10 covers the Rebbe's arrival in America on March 19, 1940 (9 Adar II) — the beginning of a new chapter that would transform American Judaism. The title means "America is no different" — the Rebbe's declaration that uncompromising Torah Judaism could thrive in the New World. Key Events Voyage to America Departure: Riga via Gothenburg, Sweden Ship: Swedish liner Drottningholm Journey: Twelve days across North Atlantic Hazards: Stopped by British warships and German submarines Arrival: Monday, March 18, 4:00 PM in New York Harbor Disembarkation: Tuesday, March 19, 1940 (9 Adar II) at Pier 97, West 57th Street First Words on American Soil Upon being welcomed by US Chabad leadership, the Rebbe immediately turned to practical matters: "The suffering I endured in prison in Russia does not compare to the torments of the twelve weeks I spent under their [Nazi] rule. Now we will quickly take care of the formalities and get to work immediately. Our work is Torah and Yiddishkeit." He quoted from morning prayers: "Hashem is hanoten laya'eif koach — the One who gives strength to those who are weary" and "al kol neshimah veneshimah tehallel Kah — we must give thanks and praise to G-d for every breath." Welcome at the Pier Crowd: Thousands of Jews of all types Press: Der Tog wrote the pier could have hosted Agudas Harabonim convention Diversity: "Young and old, rich and poor, bearing the traditional kaftan, black hats, beards and peyos, or the most elaborate creations of Parisian courtiers" Impact on Arriving Youth Yitzchok Groner (age 16): Sent by Rebbe to Australia in 1947, led Chabad there until 2008 Risya Kazarnovsky (age 13): Became pioneering emissary with husband Rabbi Zalman Posner in Nashville, 1949 The Mission Rebbe's Vision for America The ten-day voyage from Riga to New York was the Rebbe's "Ten Days of Penitence and decision-making about the kind of life that I would have to undertake." His mission: "The task of bringing to America the mesirat nefesh (self-sacrifice) for unadulterated Torah study that my revered father implanted in Tomchei Temimim." Goal: "America should replace Europe as a center of Torah." As the Young Israel Viewpoint wrote: "With his arrival the center of gravity of Jewish life shifts from the old world to the new." Previous American Experience The 1929-30 visit had prepared the way: Understanding of American Jewish landscape Established support network Knowledge of "lay of the land" Broad vision for impact Historical Significance The Rebbe's arrival marked: Transfer of Chabad leadership to America Beginning of transformation of American Torah Judaism Foundation for global Jewish outreach movement New era of post-war Jewish revitalization Key Theme "America Iz Nisht Aderesh" — The Rebbe's declaration that America was spiritually no different from Europe; authentic Torah Judaism could and must thrive in the New World. Sources Chapter 10 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S010-undaunted-chapter9]] — Chapter 9 Next: [[S012-undaunted-chapter11]] — Chapter 11

title: Undaunted - Chapter 11: A Global Vision
content: Undaunted - Chapter 11: A Global Vision Overview Chapter 11 covers the post-war period beginning in May 1945 — the Rebbe's response to the Holocaust tragedy and his vision for creating a global Jewish renaissance through scattered refugees and Chabad students worldwide. The Immediate Aftermath (May 1945) Rebbe's Grief The war ended in Europe in May 1945. In his office, the Rebbe sat gazing at pictures of his Chasidim murdered by the Nazis: Rabbi Yechezkel "Chatche" Feigin Rabbi Yitzchak "Itche der Masmid" Horowitz Many others "Uncontrollable tears flowed from the Rebbe's eyes as he looked at their faces." The fate of his daughter Sheina and her husband remained unknown. Post-War Challenges Poland: Became a graveyard; Jews not welcome back (Kielce pogrom killed 42 Jews in 1946) Refugees: Scattered across Europe, US, Canada, South America, Australia Survivors: Families and communities shattered Beachhead: Rebbe had established traditional Judaism in US since 1940 The Global Vision New Strategic Approach The Rebbe broadened his scope, believing refugees could create "Jewish revitalization on a global level." Foot soldiers for the vision: Chasidim in DP camps across Europe Students in yeshivas in Shanghai Students in US, Canada, and Israel Shanghai Students (1946) Students who had fled Poland → Lithuania → Russia → Japan → Shanghai finally arrived in US in 1946. The Rebbe's charge to them: "The reason that divine providence has brought you here is to warm up the tepid America with reverence of Heaven and chasidic passion. Proceed on the mission, with graciousness and warmth...look for the positive qualities in others and the deficiencies in yourself." Warning: "Don't get sidetracked by the nonessential." Ultimate Vision In a letter to Rabbi Yosef Wineberg: "There should be in every community and in every country, a student of Tomchei Temimim, a beacon of the Torah light in the ways of Chasidism." Refugee Camp Outreach Pocking DP Camp Shula Kazen's testimony: Chasidim escaped Russia with husband Zalman and three children Rebbe challenged refugees: "Think beyond your own needs" When Chasidim set up school for Chabad children, Rebbe asked: "Also enroll other children, both religious and secular" Chasidim were surprised — they had been mocked by secular Jews Result: "It evoked a largely positive response" Schwabisch Hall Camp Yehudis Groner's testimony: "Broken Jews with nothing" Rebbe wanted them to "spread Yiddishkeit" "Make yeshivas to educate the kids" Historical Significance This period demonstrated: Rebbe's ability to transform tragedy into mission Global perspective on Jewish rebuilding Outreach beyond traditional Chabad communities Foundation for modern shluchim (emissary) movement Key Theme From Survivors to Builders — Transforming refugee status into a mission for global Jewish revitalization, turning personal tragedy into purpose and rebuilding. Sources Chapter 11 of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S011-undaunted-chapter10]] — Chapter 10 Next: [[S013-undaunted-afterword]] — Afterword

title: Undaunted - Afterword: A Maamar in Brooklyn
content: Undaunted - Afterword: A Maamar in Brooklyn Overview The Afterword covers the first yahrzeit of the Rayatz in 1951 and the dramatic moment when the Ramash (Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson) accepted leadership to become the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. The Context First Yahrzeit (10 Shevat 1951) Date: One year after Rayatz's passing (January 1950) Location: 770 Eastern Parkway, Brooklyn Gathering: Chasidim flocked for farbrengen Question on everyone's mind: Would the Ramash become the next Rebbe? Leadership Uncertainty Unlike the Rashab who left clear instructions for his son to succeed him, the Rayatz left no directive. In the immediate wake of his passing: Some supported eldest son-in-law Rabbi Shmaryahu Gurary Many gravitated toward the Ramash Time revealed the Ramash as the clear choice The Events Leading to Acceptance Letters and Pledges For twelve months, Chasidim worldwide sent letters urging the Ramash to become Rebbe, signing a ktav hitkarut (pledge of loyalty). Private Meeting A few weeks before the yahrzeit, distinguished New York Chasidim met with the Ramash and presented a letter signed by Chasidim throughout the US declaring him the new Rebbe. The Ramash's reaction: When he read the first line, he broke down in tears, saying "This has no connection to me." Wife's Influence According to chasidic tradition, it was his wife who propelled him to his final decision, imploring: "If you don't become Rebbe, the work of my father will have been for naught." The Farbrengen (10 Shevat 1951) The Scene Time: 9:45 PM Location: Small synagogue in 770 Crowd: "Packed to the rafters" The Ramash Speaks He began by speaking about strengthening the bond between Rebbe and Chasidim: "Each of us must continue the task the Rebbe entrusted us with." He explored ahavat Yisrael — the responsibility to love every Jew, the central mitzvah. The Dramatic Moment The crowd grew restless. Would the Ramash say a maamar as his predecessors did when becoming Rebbe? Suddenly, Rabbi Sender Nemtzov — over eighty years old, former student in Lubavitch yeshivah, rabbi in Manchester for many years — rose and proclaimed: "We want the Rebbe to say chasidut [a maamar]!" Absolute silence filled the room as all eyes turned to the Ramash. Acceptance To everyone's delight, the new Rebbe began his first maamar. He started by referencing the last maamar the Rayatz had published on the eve of his passing: "My father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, writes as follows in the maamar that he released for the day of his passing, the tenth of Shevat 5710 (1950), [based on the verse in Song of Songs]: 'I have come into my garden, my sister, my bride.'" The Celebration After the first part of the maamar, the Ramash asked everyone to say lechaim. Rabbi Nemtzov — despite his advanced age — leaped onto a table and called out: "Chasidim, we have a Rebbe!" before proclaiming Shehechiyanu. The Continuity The Motif This became the motif of the seventh Rebbe's leadership: building on the foundation established by the Rayatz, expanding Chabad into the largest Jewish movement in the world. Time and again, he referred to the teachings and directives of his father-in-law as his guiding light. The Legacy Forty-two years later in 1994, when the seventh Rebbe passed away, he was interred alongside his father-in-law at the Ohel. Today, hundreds of thousands flock to pray at this holy site every year. The two graves are enveloped by stone walls that rise up to the open sky. Two simple granite headstones mark the resting place of the two great leaders of modern Jewish life who transformed Jewish destiny and created a spiritual renaissance that continues to reach every community in the world. Historical Significance This moment marked: Transition from sixth to seventh Rebbe Beginning of global Chabad expansion Continuity of spiritual leadership Foundation for modern Jewish outreach movement Key People Person Role Significance The Ramash Son-in-law of Rayatz Became seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe Rabbi Sender Nemtzov Chasidic elder Proclaimed acceptance of leadership Rebbetzin Chaya Mushka Daughter of Rayatz, wife of Ramash Influenced decision to accept leadership Rabbi Shmaryahu Gurary Eldest son-in-law Initial support for leadership faded Sources Afterword of "Undaunted" Previous: [[S012-undaunted-chapter11]] — Chapter 11 End of "Undaunted"

title: Toldot Chabad bi-Rusya ha-Tzarit - Preface
content: Toldot Chabad bi-Rusya ha-Tzarit - Preface Overview תולדות חב"ד ברוסיא הצארית (History of Chabad in Tsarist Russia) is a comprehensive scholarly work documenting the first generation of Chabad-Lubavitch leadership during the Tsarist period. This volume focuses primarily on the Alter Rebbe, Rabbi Shneur Zalman of Liadi (c. 1745-1812), founder of the Chabad movement. Key Topics Covered Halachic Works שלחן ערוך (Shulchan Aruch) - Orach Chaim - The Alter Rebbe's code of Jewish law קונטרסי הלכות (Kuntreisim) - Halachic booklets on various topics שו"ת צמח צדק (Responsa Tzemach Tzedek) - Rabbinic correspondence הלכות נטילת ידים (Laws of Handwashing) - Example of halachic methodology Historical Period Years covered: Approximately 1777-1797 (תקל"ז - תקנ"ז) Locations: Mohilev, Vitebsk, Liadi, Lubavitch Key events: The Alter Rebbe's travels, settlement in different communities The Document Structure The work is organized into 656 chapters covering: The Alter Rebbe's halachic writings and publication history His activities and influence in Russia Interactions with authorities (including arrests in 1798 and 1801) Establishment of educational institutions Community leadership and correspondence Key Figures Person Role Period [[Alter Rebbe]] Founder of Chabad 1745-1812 Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk Leader of aliyah Contemporary The Mitteler Rebbe Successor 1773-1827 The Tzemach Tzedek Grandson, author of responsa 1789-1866 Geographic Locations מאהליב (Mohilev) - Where Alter Rebbe stayed 1777-1778 ויטבסק (Vitebsk) - Major Chabad center לאדי (Liadi) - Alter Rebbe's final settlement לובאוויטש (Lubavitch) - Later became Chabad capital Sources Volume 1 of "תולדות חב"ד" series Published by ליובאוויטש ליוברערי (Lubavitch Library) This is a Hebrew language source documenting the foundational period of Chabad-Lubavitch history.

title: Toldot Chabad bi-Polin, Lita u-Latvia (Volume 5)
content: Toldot Chabad bi-Polin, Lita u-Latvia (Volume 5) Overview תולדות חב"ד בפולין, ליטא ולטביא (History of Chabad in Poland, Lithuania, and Latvia) is Volume 5 of the comprehensive Toldot Chabad series. This scholarly work by שלום דובער לוין (Shalom Dovber Levine) documents Chabad history in these regions, primarily covering the interwar period (1920-1945) and including earlier history from the Alter Rebbe's time. Written: 11 Nisan 5771 (April 2011) Published by: ספריית ליובאוויטש (Lubavitch Library) Primary Focus Tomchei Tmimim Yeshiva Network The book's main focus is the Tomchei Tmimim yeshiva network: Warsaw (1921) - Beginning of the network Vilna (1941) - Pre-war expansion Radom Ghetto (1942) - Tragic continuation during Holocaust Kobe, Japan - Exile community Shanghai, China - Refugee yeshiva Geographic Sections 1. חב"ד בליטא (Chabad in Lithuania) Historical Chabad communities in Lithuania Chabad synagogues and institutions Activity during the Rebbe Rayatz period 2. חב"ד בלטביא (Chabad in Latvia) Settlement of Chabad chassidim in Latvia Activity during the Riga period (1927-1934) Communities and correspondences 3. חב"ד בפולין (Chabad in Poland) Warsaw as major center Otwock yeshiva Pre-war community development Historical Context Period Covered: 1920-1945 (interwar + WWII) This was the period when Chabad's center was located in these countries (1928-1940) following the Bolshevik crackdown in Russia. The book documents: Chabad emissaries (shluchim) and mashpi'im (influencers) Lists of yeshiva students and staff Memorial records of those who perished in the Holocaust Archival correspondence and documents Photographs and primary source material Key Events Year Event Location 1921 Tomchei Tmimim Warsaw established Warsaw 1927 Rebbe Rayatz moves to Riga Riga, Latvia 1930s Expansion throughout Poland/Lithuania Multiple cities 1939 Nazi invasion begins Poland 1940-41 Escape routes through Japan/China Far East 1941-45 Holocaust devastation Eastern Europe Archival Value This volume serves as: Memorial to Holocaust victims from Chabad communities Historical record of interwar Chabad activities Primary source collection with letters and documents Genealogical resource with extensive name lists Sources Volume 5 of "תולדות חב"ד" series Chabad archives in Brooklyn Personal collections and testimonies Related: [[S014-toldot-chabad-russia-preface]] — Volume 1 (Tsarist Russia) Related: [[S016-toldot-chabad-artzot-habrit]] — Volume on United States

title: Toldot Chabad bi-Artzot HaBrit (History of Chabad in the United States)
content: Toldot Chabad bi-Artzot HaBrit (History of Chabad in the United States) Overview תולדות חב"ד בארצות הברית (History of Chabad in the United States) is a comprehensive historical work documenting the establishment and development of the Chabad-Lubavitch movement in America. Languages Primary: Yiddish (יידיש) Secondary: Hebrew (עברית) sections Total lines: 41,661 Key Topics Covered Early American Period (1920s-1940s) Arrival of Chabad emissaries in America Establishment of early communities Correspondence with European leadership The Rebbe Rayatz in America (1940-1950) Arrival in March 1940 (9 Adar II) Establishment of headquarters in Brooklyn (770 Eastern Parkway) Founding of American institutions Institutional Development Tomchei Temimim Yeshiva - American yeshiva network Kehot Publication Society (Otzar HaChassidim) - Publishing house Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch - Educational organizations Community organizations across multiple cities Community Development Correspondence with community members Reports on activities and growth Leadership directives and guidance Development of Chabad communities across US cities Notable Chapters Chapter Topic Chapter 2 The Meeting with the President and Farewell Party Chapter 23 About "Chabad Bulletin" publications Chapter 25 About Otzar HaChassidim (Kehot) publications Historical Significance This period (1940s onward) marked: Transfer of leadership from Europe to America Establishment of global Chabad movement from Brooklyn Founding of institutions that would enable worldwide outreach Preservation of Torah scholarship after Holocaust devastation Key Events Date Event Location 1929 Rebbe Rayatz's first American visit New York 1940, March Rebbe Rayatz arrives permanently Brooklyn 1941 Establishment of American institutions Brooklyn 1950 Passing of Rebbe Rayatz Brooklyn 1951 Seventh Rebbe accepts leadership Brooklyn Sources Part of "תולדות חב"ד" series Chabad archives Community correspondence Institutional records Contemporary reports and publications Related: [[S014-toldot-chabad-russia-preface]] — Volume on Tsarist Russia Related: [[S015-toldot-chabad-polin-lita-latvia]] — Volume 5 (Poland, Lithuania, Latvia)

title: A Prince in Prison
content: A Prince in Prison Subtitle: The Previous Lubavitcher Rebbe's Account of His Incarceration in Stalinist Russia in 1927 Author: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn (Frierdiker Rebbe / Rayatz) Source: Extract from Likkutei Dibburim Publisher: Sichos In English, Brooklyn, NY Year: 1997 (57th Anniversary Edition) ISBN: 1-8814-0027-1 Overview This document is the Frierdiker Rebbe's first-person account of his arrest, imprisonment, and liberation by Soviet authorities in 1927. It was originally written as Reshimas HaMaasar ("An Account of the Imprisonment") and completed in Riga on 15 Sivan, 5688 (1928) — the first anniversary of his arrest. The work appears as part of Volume IV of Likkutei Dibburim and documents the Rebbe's resistance to Stalin's ideological suppression of Jewish life, for which he "spearheaded the underground Jewish resistance" and paid with "brutal incarceration and a capital sentence." Structure of the Work The published volume contains four main parts plus an appendix: Part I: Sections 1-17 — The Imprisonment of 1927 Details the arrest from June 15, 1927 (15 Sivan 5687) at 2:15 AM through the imprisonment period. Describes: Midnight arrest by GPU agents in Leningrad The search of the Rebbe's home Interrogation and prison conditions Divine perspective on suffering Part II: Sections 1-20 — The Imprisonment of 1927 Continuation of the imprisonment narrative Part III: Sections 1-15 — The Imprisonment of 1927 Further details, followed by Sections 16-26: Chronicles of the First Three Generations of Chabad Chassidism Part IV: Sections 1-25 — Recollections of Childhood The Rebbe's memories between ages 6-11, being groomed by his father (Rashab) to succeed him as Rebbe Appendix: Prisons are Transient The Rebbe's farewell message at the train station, 3 Tammuz 5687, upon departure to exile in Kostroma Key Events Documented Arrest Timeline (1927) Date Event 15 Sivan (June 14) Arrest at 2:15 AM in Leningrad 15 Sivan - 3 Tammuz 18 days, 11 hours, 15 minutes of imprisonment 3 Tammuz, 1:30 PM Capital charge quashed; sentenced to exile instead 3 Tammuz, 7:30 PM Departure by train to Kostroma 4 Tammuz Arrival in Kostroma 12-13 Tammuz Notification of liberation; actual release Seven Terms of Imprisonment The Rebbe documents that the 1927 imprisonment was his seventh: First (1892): Lubavitch, age 11 — recorded in his recollections book Second (1902): Lubavitch, Iyar 5662 — informers from Haskalah school Third (1906): Lubavitch, Teves 5666 — Poalei Tzion uprising Fourth (1910): Petrograd, Teves 5670 — informer: educated Jew "K" Fifth (1916): Petrograd, Shvat 5676 — military exemption advocacy Sixth (1920): Rostov on Don, Tammuz 5680 — denounced by Yevsektsia head "D" Seventh (1927): Leningrad — capital sentence, commuted to exile, then liberation Key Themes Mesirus Nefesh (Self-Sacrifice) The Rebbe writes: "A period rich in suffering which is inflicted on account of his vigorous endeavors for an ideal... escalates a man's personality and gifts. Such days are the luminous days in a man's life." Divine Perspective "Normally, an analogy is less earnest than its analog. Consider, then: If imprisoning a body in a jail of wood and stone is called suffering, then how intense must be the suffering of the Divine soul when it is imprisoned in the body and the animal soul." Transience vs. Eternity From the farewell message: "We must always bear in mind that prisons and hard-labor camps are transient, whereas the Torah, its mitzvos, and the Jewish people, are eternal." Childhood Recollections (Part IV) The fourth section contains vivid memories of the young Yosef Yitzchak (ages 6-11): Education: Relationship with teacher R. Shneur Slonim Family dynamics: Learning about his grandfather (Maharash)'s instruction that his sons never engage in business, only Torah study First Yom Tov experiences: Staying awake for Shavuos Tikkun Trips to Mozinkes: Accompanying his father to the forest resort Kharkov farbrengen: Witnessing his father's farbrengen as a young child, being passed from chassid to chassid Emotional sensitivity: Deep connection to his mother's feelings Historical Context The GPU/NKVD The document provides contemporary witness to the Stalinist secret police operations: Midnight arrests without warrants Interrogation tactics and intimidation Prison conditions and humiliation Use of informers within the Jewish community (Yevsektsia) Jewish Underground Resistance The publisher's foreword notes that the Rebbe "spearheaded the underground Jewish resistance to Stalin's ideological Final Solution" — referring to the systematic suppression of Jewish religious life. Companion Volume Defiance and Devotion (Kehot, 1996) is described as an "invaluable companion" — it contains selected discourses (maamarim) delivered by the Rebbe Rayatz during the arrest and liberation period, calling on chassidim to defy the Soviet regime "even at the cost of literal self-sacrifice." Sources and References The document references numerous historical sources: Sefer HaSichos 5680-5687 — contemporary documents Sefer HaToldos of the Rebbe Rayatz (4 vols., Rabbi Chanoch Glitzenstein) Toldos Chabad BeRussia HaSovietis (Rabbi Shalom Dovber Levin) HaTamim journal — publications of the Rebbe's letters Likkutei Sichos — the Rebbe's teachings Notable Quotes On the nature of suffering: "Every single incident in such a period is significant... For every hour and minute of torment gives rise to inestimable benefits: it makes a man so resolute that even a weakling is transformed into the most courageous of men." On his arrest: "I answered coolly and clearly: 'I don't know which Schneersohn you are looking for... As to the officers of the said administration, I have not feared them, I do not fear them, and I shall not fear them.'" Farewell message (3 Tammuz 1927): "Nevertheless, all the nations on the face of the earth must know: Our bodies alone were banished into exile to be ruled by the nations of the world. Our souls were never exiled, nor were they subjected to the rule of the nations." See Also [[C001-yud-beis-tammuz]] — Liberation day [[E001-rayatz]] — The Rebbe Rayatz [[E002-rashab]] — His father, the Fifth Rebbe [[E007-the-rebbe]] — His son-in-law, who published these accounts [[I002-tomchei-temimim]] — The yeshivah founded by Rashab

title: The Heroic Struggle: The Arrest and Liberation of Rabbi Yosef Y. Schneersohn
content: The Heroic Struggle: The Arrest and Liberation of Rabbi Yosef Y. Schneersohn of Lubavitch Source: Kehot Publication Society, 1999. Translated and adapted from the memoirs of the Rebbe by Rabbi Dr. Alter B. Z. Metzger. Overview This comprehensive work documents the 1927 arrest, imprisonment, and liberation of the Frierdiker Rebbe, Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn, by the Soviet G.P.U. (secret police). The book is a primary source compilation that includes: Reshimat Hama'aser (Notes of the Arrest) — the Rebbe's autobiographical account written on 15 Sivan 5688, one year after his arrest Yagdil Torah journal account of the prison cell experiences Di Yisurim fun Lubavitcher Rebben in Soviet Russland (Riga 1930) — covering the first interrogation through liberation Recently declassified G.P.U. files Eyewitness accounts from chassidim including Reb Eliyahu Chaim Althaus Historical background on Soviet anti-religious campaigns Historical Context Soviet War on Yiddishkeit The book provides extensive background on the systematic Communist campaign to eliminate Jewish religious observance: 1917: Bolshevik Revolution promises "abolition of national and religious disabilities" 1918: Creation of Yevkom (Commissariat for Jewish National Affairs) and Yevsektzia (Jewish Sections of Communist Party) 1919: Kehillah (Jewish communal structure) abolished 1921: First anti-Jewish trials begin; cheder of Vitebsk and Lubavitcher yeshivah in Rostov pronounced illegal 1922: Sunday established as day of rest 1929: Decree mandates no cessation of work and no common day of rest Key figures in the anti-religious campaign: Semyon (Shimon) Dimanshtein — head of Yevkom, formerly traditionally educated Samuel (Shmuel) Agurskii — deputy commissar who signed kehillah abolition decree Moishe Litvakov — former Hebrew cantor, editor of Der Emes, possessed large collection of Hebrew books on Chassidus Maria Yakovleva Frumkina (Esther) — leader in Yevsektzia, granddaughter of a rabbi, author of "Down with the Rabbis" All were later liquidated during Stalin's purges of 1936-1938. The Va'ad Organization Founded in 1922 with the Rebbe at its head, this underground organization: Organized jurist legal advisory board that restored many synagogues Intervened when Gomel Jewish cemetery was expropriated (Trotsky personally telegrammed to stop it) Mounted intensive petition campaign for religious education Funded education of 5,000 children (16,000 had to be refused for lack of funds) Established small number of yeshivot providing room, board, and clothing Set up advanced house of study to train rabbis and shochatim Maintained "Tiferet Bachurim" night classes for workers and professionals Chronology of Events Prelude to Arrest (1920-1927) Tammuz 5680 (1920): Yevsektzia agents enter Rebbe's synagogue with pistols drawn; Rebbe declares: "I have already declared that I will not abandon my principles. No man or demon has been born, nor will be born that will make me budge even slightly." Iyar 5681 (1921): Libelous campaign produces decree to close Rebbe's yeshivah; all property confiscated Nissan 5684 (1924): Rebbe returns to Rostov, visited by soldiers proposing voluntary imprisonment; after negotiations, allowed to leave for Leningrad Adar 5684 (1924): Rebbe elected leader of Moscow organization; makes binding "covenant" with nine temimim to work "even to the last drop of blood" 5685-5686 (1925-1926): Terror weakens somewhat; Rebbe establishes yeshivot to teach Chassidus Rosh Hashanah 5687 (1927): Number of visitors exceeds capacity of Rebbe's home June 1927: Wave of executions and terror across Russia; foreign diplomats assassinated The Arrest Date: Tuesday night, 14 Sivan 5687 (June 14, 1927), shortly after midnight The arrest came after the Rebbe had concluded receiving people for private audiences (which ran from 7 PM to 11:30 PM). Key G.P.U. officials present: Nachmanson — young Jewish G.P.U. official from Nevel, son of a chassid who had traveled to Lubavitch Lulav — short dark-haired Jewish man from Riga family The Rebbe's response to the armed G.P.U. agents: "I have not feared, I do not fear, and I will not fear them." His daughters Chaya Moussia and Shaina defiantly told Nachmanson they were "members of our father's party" and would not be intimidated. Imprisonment at Spalerno Duration: 19 days (14 Sivan - 3 Tammuz) The notorious Spalerno prison in Leningrad was the site of: Harsh interrogations Psychological torture witnessing prisoners taken out to be shot The Rebbe's unwavering faith and dignity Despite conditions, the Rebbe: Put on tefillin and prayed daily Maintained his composure and principles Refused to sign false documents Exile to Kostroma 3 Tammuz (July 3): After 19 days in Spalerno, told he could return home for 6 hours, then must take train to Kostroma for 3-year exile. The Rebbe's parting words at the train station: "Only our bodies were sent into exile and subjugated to alien rule; our souls were not given over into captivity and foreign rule... No one can impose his belief upon us, nor coerce us to conduct ourselves contrary to our beliefs." In Kostroma: Rebbe arrived late night, 4 Tammuz Reported to G.P.U. next morning; told he was "an exile-prisoner" and "criminal receiving punishment" Rebbe Michoel Dworkin had already arrived, established a cheder and fixed the mikveh The shochet of Kostroma provided lodging The Liberation 12 Tammuz (July 12): The Rebbe's 47th birthday — the day of liberation The local G.P.U. official informed him: "You are totally freed from the need of any further appearances. The order has been received to grant you full freedom." Upon hearing the news, Rebbe Michoel Dworkin danced around the house singing "Nyet, nyet nikago" — "Nothing exists aside from G-d!" The Rebbe delivered the ma'amar "G-d is among those that help me" that day. 13 Tammuz: Received Certificate of Release; delivered ma'amar "Baruch HaGomel" (Blessed be the One Who bestows good upon the unworthy) 14 Tammuz: Left Kostroma as free citizen 15 Tammuz: Arrived home in Leningrad; called to Torah (Parshat Pinchas) and recited HaGomel blessing Departure from Russia Following continued threats from Lulav, Nachmanson, and Messing, the Rebbe moved to Malachovka (near Moscow) and eventually left Russia entirely — with the help of international diplomatic pressure including: Dr. Hildesheimer and Dr. Leo Baeck of Berlin Madam Peshkova of the Political Prisoner Relief organization The Frankfurt Jewish community's invitation Key Themes Mesirus Nefesh (Self-Sacrifice) The Rebbe's unwavering commitment to Yiddishkeit despite: Threats of imprisonment and death Physical and psychological torture Pressure to abandon principles Witnessing executions of fellow prisoners Faith Under Duress The Rebbe maintained: Daily prayer with tefillin Refusal to sign false documents Dignified defiance in face of terror Spiritual leadership even from prison International Solidarity The liberation resulted from: World outcry at the arrest Diplomatic pressure from abroad Petitions and prayers worldwide Media coverage in Western press Spiritual Impact The Rebbe reflected years later: "I lost all sense of gratification that is derived from material things, not only for a while, but permanently. Then I did not think of myself at all... I never experienced a sense of solitude; I was always mindful of the fact that I possessed revered ancestors." No man or demon has been born, nor will be born that will make me budge even slightly." — 1920 confrontation with Yevsektzia "Only our bodies were sent into exile and subjugated to alien rule; our souls were not given over into captivity and foreign rule." — Parting address at train station, 3 Tammuz "We did not depart from the Land of Israel of our own free will, nor shall we return to the Land of Israel by virtue of our own capabilities. G-d, our Father and King, has sent us into exile." "I lost all sense of gratification that is derived from material things, not only for a while, but permanently." — Reflection many years later Last Updated: 2026-04-13 Wiki Status: Complete
